speaker: interviewer
text: Roll number 2.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mark, you were telling us about your father, and how you were separated from him. And-- and he was trying to liquidate things. Tell us about that.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. So I was still having some hopes that, eventually, my father would come, and we would be together again. But it became an impossible thing. Because on that river [NON-ENGLISH] I mentioned to you before, the Germans were killing everybody they saw trying to cross the river. And I imagine my father, may be-- maybe he was one of them. I don't know. But I never heard from him. And I never saw him again, never.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did-- did he explain to you, though, what was happening with the situation? Did you understand what was going on?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not really. Because everything happened so quickly. Nobody realized that Poland was occupied in a couple of weeks. And in those couple of weeks, hundreds of thousands of people were killed already. So like I said, this was a blitzkrieg. Nobody had even a chance to-- to do anything.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you aware, before all this happened--

speaker: interviewee
text: Excuse me, I'd like  to add one thing, too. To give you just an idea how-- how chaotic things were, Polish government didn't even have a chance to mobilize me in the army. When the war started in 1939, September 1st, normally speaking, I would be in the Polish Army, you know. It couldn't happen. Poland was practically wiped off in no time at all-- almost.

speaker: interviewer
text: But prior to that-- we talked a little bit about the anti-Semitism. What did you see or what-- what were you aware of, as far as anti-Semitism, before all this happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: Where all this happened?

speaker: interviewer
text: Before all this happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: Before the war?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes. You were aware of anti-Semetism?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes. I personally was maybe a little bit fortunate, because I didn't personally suffer that much from anti-Semitism. In order to clarify this, I must-- I must-- I must make some comment on the life in  Poland-- a brief one. You see, most of the Jewish people in Poland were really living on the fringe of the Polish society. Now there are reasons for it, just like there are for anything. The number one reason was anti-Semitism, which didn't really allow many Jews to hold positions. You know, all kinds of jobs, they couldn't get. On the other hand, this caused the Jews to isolate themselves from the rest of the population, from the Poles. And they-- they never quite became-- the majority of the Jewish people-- we're talking about three million people in Poland-- three million Jews-- never quite became part of the Polish society. Anti-Semitism is one reason. The other reason is-- see, it is a story with-- sometimes, you can compare this with the chicken and an egg. Nobody knows what started first. Because of anti-Semitism, Jews isolated themselves, and they had to seek other ways of survival, you know. They couldn't get a lot of jobs which the Poles where able to get.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they turned to commerce, you know. Many Poles became terribly anti-Semitic, because when they saw 1 or 2 or 10 Jews  who were maybe dishonest, they branded, automatically, all the Jews being crooks. You know what I mean? That's the story. We suffered with this for years, through generations.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's why I don't like people who say, all the Poles are anti-Semites, because that's just as stupid as-- as the Poles who say all the Jews are crooks. It's not such-- and even among Germans, there were some Germans who were decent, believe it or not. So one can not put everybody in the same. It's just unfair, you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: But there's no doubt about it, that the majority of the Polish population was anti-Semitic, for whatever reasons. But like I said, the Church had a lot to do with this. They played a major role as a generator of anti-Semitism in Poland. You know what I mean? Because of their-- of what they were trying to brainwash the people in the belief that the Jews are responsible for Christ's death. Jews are responsible for this. Everything that happened that is bad in Poland is because of the Jews. You know what I mean? So that happened in Poland. Not much different being said. As a matter of fact, Hitler, when he came to power in 1933, he was trying to brainwash the German people into a belief that the Jews have a lot to do with the fact that German suffers an economic [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] deteriorations or whatever. When the war ended, in 1918, you know, the Germans never accepted the defeat. They never could believe that they actually lost the war, you know? Because I want you to know that the Germans, before Hitler-- many Jewish people told me that-- and my father told me that-- that the Germans were on best terms with the Jews.

speaker: interviewee
text: Jews in Germany enjoyed all the freedoms. As a  matter of fact, Jews in Germany felt much better about themselves than the Jews in Poland felt about themselves. Because the Germans being not Catholic but Protestant, you know, Protestant religion is, in quite substantial ways, different from Catholics. And so far as the Jews are concerned, the Protestants were much friendlier to Jews than the Catholics ever were.

speaker: interviewer
text: So in Germany, Jews felt a lot better than in Poland. That's why this was such a shock for the Jews in Germany to-- to believe that the Germans could become not just anti-Semitic but murderers. They became professional murderers. That's what Hitler did. You know, he was brainwashing the Germans into the belief that Jews are the cause of all the economic problems they had in Germany.

speaker: interviewee
text: As a matter of fact, in times of Tsar in Russia, the Tsar was  also trying  to do this. Jews are always the scapegoat. Whatever goes wrong is because of the Jews. Now, I'd like to go back. I think you would like me to go back to the-- to the original thing.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, I would like to ask you, when September 1st, 1939, came around, and you left your father during that time-frame.

speaker: interviewee
text: Because on my father's insistence, he wanted to save me.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did he say to you?

speaker: interviewee
text: That I must go, because my-- my life is so much in danger, which it was. Because the young people were immediately taken into concentration camps and all that. The older people-- first, they started with the young. So my father said, you go first, and then I will meet you somewhere in the eastern part of Poland. But that never happened. And I never stopped feeling guilty about that, either. I want to tell you what happened. When I actually reached, already, the eastern part of Poland, I told you, eventually, the Russians let us through. And they didn't allow us to stay there. Because they told-- hear  this. They told me that, either you go back to the Nazis, or you go in deep Russia. And of course, nobody wanted to go to the Nazis. So they transported us in freight trains to a town called Penza, which is still a European part of Russia, quite a bit away from the border. I mean there was no danger there at all, because it was far from the  front line, from Germany.

speaker: interviewee
text: Over there, in Penza, I was working there as-- I don't even remember what kinds of-- some sort of slave jobs. But our lives were not threatened the same way as they were threatened under the Nazis. You  know what I mean? There was no political-- anything like that. But we were just hungry. And we had to do a task, which even people who are well-fed could hardly put up with. What can I tell you? Did you to-- did you try to keep in touch with your father?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I was, but there was no contact. Absolutely nothing. Maybe the mail didn't even get through. I don't believe-- would the Germans care about, you know, enabling one Jew to contact another Jew? That was out of the question. Now, that was my time between 1939 and '40. Actually, it was about a year I spent there  in Penza. Then I never gave up on the idea of reaching my father somehow. I said to myself, I have to get out from this part of Russia. Maybe I will find a way of going back to Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I found a way to go back to Poland, but not to Warsaw, of course. I wanted to be as close as I possibly could to Warsaw, which is a town, again, in Belarus, Bialystok. Why Bialystok? Because I had an aunt in Bialystok. She was a sister of my mother, who was working in a clinic. What was her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Her name was Renia, which is, in Polish spelling, is R-E-N-I-A. She was assisting the doctors there. It was an eye clinic, very famous eye clinic-- eye clinic in Bialystok, called [INAUDIBLE] Pines, P-I-N-E-S. He was a famous ophthalmologist, you  know. And he had a little clinic that my aunt was working for him. But when I got back to Bialystok, I couldn't find him. There was no clinic. It was all destroyed. And the Russians, who occupied Bialystok at the time-- as I told  you, according to the secret agreement, that part of Poland belonged to Russia-- they wouldn't let me live in Bialystok. They said, you have to go further east. This is too close to the-- to the border.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then I wound up in a town called Baranovichi. Should I spell it? If it is important? It's close to Bialystok. Baranovichi, and I was teaching there. I learned Russian, already, on my first trip to Russian. And I was able to teach some little kids the Russian language. The kids were Belarusians, you know? They spoke-- they had their own language, which is very similar to Russian. But I picked up the Russian language, very quickly. So I taught them the Russian, because they had to learn Russian. They were under Russian occupation. And all the other subjects, children, five, six years old and all that.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then, I will never forget, it was 1941, June the 22nd. As I was sitting in the library, and sitting, and reading something in Baranovichi, which was a nice-sized town, all of a sudden, I see the German planes-- the Messerschmitt-- bombarding everything. And here, I hear the announcement, on the radio, in the library, that the war broke out between Russia and Germany, which was a totally unexpected, shocking experience. Because, a year ago, they were pretending to be friends. Imagine. But Hitler had his plans a long time ago. And the Russians didn't know it. He didn't stop on Poland. He wanted to destroy the world, including this country. As a matter of fact, he had it planned.

speaker: interviewer
text: So the war broke out. And I was near a field of rye and whatever was growing there, you know. It was a time of harvest-- June 22, hot. And I was hiding there. And a lot of people got killed, instantaneously, you know. The guy from the plane was shooting just like that, the Messerschmitt, which is the German planes. And I was wounded. Now, would you ever believe that the fact that I was wounded saved my life? You say, how is that possible, right? See, we always talked about logic. There are many things which defy logic completely. I mean that doesn't make any sense. But it made sense in my particular circumstances. I'll tell you why. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: As I was wounded, I was, of course, fully covered with blood and all that. There was a few Russian ambulances there. After the attack of the German planes, they were looking through these fields and searching for bodies. And I was lying there, all bloody, and had a big hole here  in my arm, which I still have the mark of it. And when they saw this, they took me to the-- to their ambulance. And they dressed up the wound. And they put me on a freight train, which was going deep in Russia, and I mean deep. And that saved my life. If I weren't wounded, I would be either left there or be killed. So the fact that I was wounded saved my life. And, of course, on my way to Russia, the freight train was making several stops. And on each stop, they were changing my dressing. And eventually, I wound up in a small town called Sarapul-- Sarapul-- way out, thousands and thousands of miles deep in Russia. That is one time I appreciated the size of Russia, very well. Because I was very far from the German front lines. And the best place one could be in that time was a hospital. They took me to the hospital. And the doctor, all the people in there are the red-hairs. They all have red-hairs in this particular province. And the doctor, her name was Protopopova. I will never forget this as long as  live, because she saved my life.

speaker: interviewer
text: And why I said the hospital was the best place? Because it was the only place where they had food. In hospital they had food. Every house was starving. Everybody else was starving. It was the wartime. The war was still going on between Russia and Germany, but the hospital had to have some minimum food.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I didn't want to go out. I wanted to stay in this hospital as long as possible. So they-- they fed me. I had bread. I had soup. Even occasionally I had a little piece of meat, which was unbelievable, considering the fact that, in most of the country, people were starving. And I was there pretty much till the end of the war, practically. Well, I mean, not in the hospital. But I was-- after I was released from the  hospital, I was working. They tell you-- I would like to tell you one of my work experiences in Russia, which can happen only in Russian, nowhere else. They had a lot of those camps, they called forced labor camps-- which are not to be confused or compared in any way to the German concentration camps. But we were also led to work with bayonets and dogs and all that, like some sort of criminals. They never trusted me, because I told you, I mentioned  to you before, I was from Warsaw. People from Warsaw could not be trusted, because we were suspected of being Nazi spies, which is total nonsense. But you couldn't talk to the communists. I mean, whatever they said, that was it. Now, here is the job they gave me. There is a River Kama, which is the biggest tributary to the River Volga. You've heard of Volga. It's a huge, tremendous river. Kama is also very big. Now they were trying to-- I never remember the word in English for-- You know, when you send your wood, construction logs, lots of wood, through the water, the river, and big, big barges, you know. And Russia has huge distances. It's tremendous. It's beyond understanding, really. And in the meantime, that river froze. And the ice was like three, four feet thick. The wood was still stuck in that ice.

speaker: interviewer
text: So my job, and many other's job, consisted of trying to get that ice out, knock that ice out, to drag the-- the wood out, which they need-- they needed the wood for construction jobs. Now, I had no strength of  any kind to speak  of. We were starving. We were hungry. We were cold. And here, they gave me a huge-- how will you call this-- a metal, heavy metal like a stick. I don't know   how to call this in English, would you believe  it? Just picture  a broomstick, except it is like twice-- three times as long-- and it's all heavy metal. So I had to hit the ice in order to get-- after I hit the ice, a little piece cracked, which amounted to nothing. We're talking about three feet  of ice, you know? That was my job for about three months. Incredible. Incredible. Had you lost a lot of weight by that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, my God, weight? [INAUDIBLE] We were just talking abut survival. But this may interest you. As severe as the climate is  in most of Russia-- especially in this part of Russia-- I couldn't get sick. And I wanted to get sick. If you get-- if you got a fever or something, you'd get a day off from work, you know, which was like being in a paradise. I couldn't get sick. Well, partly because probably I was young. And you know, when you are young, you can overcome this kind of a thing. And secondly, see, the climate is great. It's cold, but it's not a climate that makes you sick-- not me, anyway. It's a dry cold. And it's near Siberia there. I was very close to Siberia. But I was never in Siberia.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you witness any resistance over the period of the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: Did I personally-- in Russia, being in  Russia, you mean?

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, no. Actually, did you witness any of that happening when you were in Germany?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No. I was with the Germans only a couple of days, like I told you. I-- I was, in a way, lucky. I was not-- except for the two days that they sadistically tortured us, really. But--

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, yeah, did I mention that to you? Oh, my god, I don't remember if I. When I was talking about going to Zamosc, trying to reach my relatives in Tomaszow, Zamosc was overtaken by the Germans, after a very short fight with the Poles. But then the next day, there was an agreement between Germany and Russia, in accordance with which Zamosc belonged to Russia.

speaker: interviewee
text: And that, again, was another lifesaving situation. Because if I were with Germans another couple of days, I would be killed, no question about it. I would be  in some concentration camp, or something like that. That  would be the end. But because of that agreement, Zamosc was taken over by the Russians.

speaker: interviewer
text: And that was a lifesaving thing. I mean, when we saw the Russian tanks, and the Russia soldiers, everybody was kissing them. They saved our lives. But we couldn't remain there. And we didn't want to remain there, because how could anybody know if the next week they will change their plans again? You-- you know what I mean? It was a totally unbelievable situation. Every one of us who survived is by sheer miracle. Like I told you, who would know that you had to be wounded to remain alive? So-- so you were working on the ice.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you go after that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, eventually-- I was doing this  like for about three or four months. I had another job in Russia, which was running a tractor. They taught me. They trained me to be a tractor operator, you know. They needed people driving tractors. They gave me an old, terrible jalopy. At-- at one point, I remembered that I was trying to ignite the thing, and it bounced back at me, because it was one of those hand-- hand-cranked thing. It was before your times, believe me. But the cars, the early cars used to have this. Maybe you know that from reading. You didn't have a thing, an ignition you put a key, and the car starts. That-- that wasn't true for many years. You had to use a crank and-- and in order to start the ignition. So that was the way the tractor operated. Except a tractor is a lot heavier machine. So if you get, like, premature ignition, the club goes, the crank goes back, and can kill you. And it almost did in my case.

speaker: interviewer
text: So that was what I had to do. I was-- I had to run this tractor machine. They taught me. There was a special class. And I was pretty good at  it. At least running the tractor gave me an opportunity to have some more bread, because what we were doing, it was like a stationary machine. I didn't go anywhere with this tractor. We were milling, you know, like when you have a  mill. You make flour. You know what I mean? And that machine was connected with the other machine. And the tractor's engine was running it-- you know what I mean-- and creating flour, milling, you know. It was like a milling machine. Like-- So that was my job. I had to sit there all day and watch the tractor [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewee
text: I even had a job, once, which was considered a miracle, for about three or four weeks, in a bakery. In time of war, when people were starving to death, working in a bakery, surrounded by all-- by all that bread was being in a paradise. You could eat all you wanted while you there, but you were not allowed to take   a crumb of bread out. If they catch you taking a slice of bread, they kill you. That was the law. And the moment-- the way things happen. You know, while you're there, you see all the bread around you, and you're not hungry. You became-- you become hungry when you leave the bakery. That's when you feel that you want to eat, which is incredible, but that's the way it is.

speaker: interviewer
text: So then the war ended in 1945, in May. And here is where I'm coming to the most interesting part. We were not-- I was not married at the time. And I had no idea at all that my wife is the general area where I am. Just by sheer miracle, she came to that area, where I was, not knowing anything about me, and she asked somebody, are there any Jewish, Polish-Jewish refugees here in this general area?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes, yes, there is a group of Poles. And she met a fellow who was a tailor. And he says, she says she gave him my name. He said, oh, yes, there is one guy here. This is his name. My god, and he works here, you know, with us. He was a tailor.

speaker: interviewer
text: And that was in that town, Sarapul. When we saw each other, it is something you can only see in the movies. Because, you know, we are talking about like 5,000 miles deep in Russia, and you are going to meet somebody you know from home, from-- from Warsaw, Poland, a former neighbor or yours, which comes from the same building? You can only see something like this in the movies, you know? So we decided that we are kind of destined for one another. And this is it. We didn't have any Jewish wedding, because we couldn't get a rabbi in Russia, at that time, anyway. Just forget about that. So we decided that we are going to be together. And-- and the war, after the war, 1945, May, there was an agreement between Russian and Polish, the new Polish government, you know, that the former Polish citizens, Poles or Jews, if they want to return to Poland, they can do so. So we already-- we immediately, sign up on that list, you know, and hoping that we will find some survivors in Poland. Ha. We came to Poland [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] in '46. We were, you know. No, before going to Poland, yes, [INAUDIBLE], before going to Poland we went to Austria. Because that's where they sent  us. We  only could go wherever they sent us. We were in a little town called Linz in Austria. And there we got a hold of a rabbi, and a whole group of us got like a common wedding, whatever you want to call it. And they gave us rings. And then we were-- I still have the [NON-ENGLISH] Do you know what that is? [NON-ENGLISH]. Do you know what that is? I still have it somewhere here. And a rabbi officiated. And we were married in accordance with the Jewish law.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then, we were trained by some people, by some Jewish organization, to become halutzim in Israel-- Palestine at that time. In other words, we were considering the possibility of going to-- to Palestine. However, nothing materialized, you know. We couldn't go there.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then-- what happened then? We talking about-- oh, yeah. Yeah. And then, of course, we were sent-- there was an organization, UNRRA, United Nations Refugee something organization, which was kind of in charge of all the refugees. We were refugees, you know, victims of the war. And they send us to Germany. And then I found myself in Ulm, which is Bavaria, southern part of Germany. By the way, Einstein was born in Ulm. And Donau-- Donau was the name of the river-- in English, Danube. 'Blue Danube,' you've heard of the waltz, 'Blue Danube,' maybe? That's in English. And in German, it's Donau. So that town, Ulm, we were in that town, living. We were sent to a German barracks, former German Army barracks called Boelcke-Kaserne. If you want  me to spell that for you, I can do that. Boelcke is-- that's the name of the barracks, B-O-E-L-C-K-E dash Kaserne-- which means barracks-- K-A-S-E-R-N-E.

speaker: interviewee
text: Now, it wasn't too bad there. They were-- those are former, you know, domiciles of the German soldiers. And it wasn't too bad, except we were terribly crowded. But we had running water, even it was possible to take a bath there. [CHUCKLES] And so we were there through '46, '47.

speaker: interviewer
text: I was working, by the way, in the employment office. I was an employment officer. Because a lot of our refugees were employed by the UNRRA. UNRRA was an organization, as I mentioned to you, the international United Nations Refugee organization. It was under the American control. And they wanted people to help them. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Mm-hm. OK? Just finish what you were saying there.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. And they wanted people to help them to organize employment office. And I was one of them. I was an employment officer. But it was all organized and sponsored by UNRRA, 1946. '47, actually, pretty much until we left for the United States, which was '49. We came--
